title: Ones to watch: KRAS degraders and inhibitors enter clinical trials
html_body:
What matters in cancer research |
View this email in your browser Thursday 16 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we learn that anti-KRAS treatments are being tested in clinical trials, discover a drug that might shrink ovarian and endometrial cancers and hear that AI misinformation was used to justify a decision to refuse cancer treatment.

Ones to watch: next-gen KRAS drugs

Mutant KRAS proteins help to drive several types of lethal tumour, including pancreatic cancer. (PJD/SPL)

KRAS drugs enter clinical trials

Several clinical trials are underway to test next-generation drugs that stop mutated KRAS proteins from driving cancer. These drugs fit into two groups: the ‘degraders’, which trick the cell into treating the mutant proteins like rubbish, and the ‘inhibitors’, which block KRAS activity. This year, the first KRAS degrader, called setidegrasib, had some success in a clinical trial. Four large clinical trials are also testing KRAS inhibitors, including the drug daraxonrasib, manufactured by Revolution Medicines.

Nature | 5 min read

Quote of the week

“It’s a nasty drug. It causes crazy stuff like my body can’t grow skin and so I bleed all out of a whole bunch of parts of me that shouldn’t be bleeding.”

Former US senator Ben Sasse has stage 4 pancreatic cancer and is experiencing ‘nuclear’-level pain as a side effect from daraxonrasib, an experimental drug he’s taking as part of a clinical trial — the same drug discussed above. (The New York Times | 1 hour listen)

Drug might shrink ovarian cancer

A drug called Mocertatug Rezetecan, or Mo-Rez, might shrink or eliminate tumours in more than 60% of individuals with endometrial cancer or chemotherapy-resistant ovarian cancer, its manufacturer GSK reports. Over the past year, the antibody–drug conjugate was trialled in 224 people with endometrial cancer or ovarian cancer where chemotherapy had failed, and showed promising results and limited side effects. GSK reported the unpublished results at a Society of Gynecologic Oncology’s annual meeting on women’s cancer in Puerto Rico. In the coming months, the drug will be tested in five, late-stage clinical trials.

The Guardian | 4 min read

Married couples have lower cancer risks

People who have never been married are more likely to get cancer than people who have been (or are) married. In a study of more than 4 million cancer cases, the risk of cancer was 68% higher in never-married men and 85% higher in never-married women. Tying the knot does not affect cancer biology in itself, but other factors, such as smoking, stress and giving birth could be linked with marriage and might drive the association with cancer, report the researchers. “It means that if you're not married, you should be paying extra attention to cancer risk factors, getting any screenings you may need, and staying up to date on health care,” says clinical psychologist Frank Penedo.

ScienceAlert | 3 min read
 Reference: Cancer Research Communications paper (8 April)

In the news

  • The US Food and Drug Administration has rejected an experimental melanoma treatment called RP1 for a second time. The regulatory body cited concerns that the effects of the virus-based treatment couldn’t be distinguished from those of the other drugs given during the clinical trial. (STAT | 3 min read)
  • Only around half of people with advanced cancers have their tumours genetically sequenced in the United States, despite this being recommended by treatment guidelines, a study of more than 63,000 people shows. (STAT | 6 min read) Reference: JAMA Network Open paper (7 April)
  • Biopharmaceutical company Gilead Sciences is acquiring the German biotechnology company Tubulis for more than US$3 billion, along with its antibody–drug conjugates for cancer. (STAT | 2 min read)

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE
Nathan Gray (left) from the St. Jude Center of Advanced Practice, and St. Jude faculty member Marcin Wlodarski (right), Department of Hematology. (St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital)

Blood test could spot leukaemia sooner

Spotting early signs of leukaemia in children can save lives — but it usually means early, invasive bone marrow exams. St. Jude investigators have shown there’s a gentler way. By analysing a simple blood sample, they detected pre-leukaemic clones with high accuracy, picking up genetic changes present in as little as 1% of cells. The approach helped clinicians intervene earlier in at-risk patients, and could complement or reduce reliance on bone marrow sampling. While not perfect for every chromosomal change, the study supports a more frequent, less invasive surveillance strategy for high-risk children.

Reference: Blood paper

Features & opinion

Misled by AI, father refuses treatment

Ben Riley, a self-taught cognitive scientist, created a newsletter to ‘explain AI to the average Joe’. His father, Joe, was one of his first subscribers. Ben was therefore shocked — and could see the irony — when he discovered that his dad, a retired neuroscientist, had been using the artificial intelligence chatbot Perplexity to justify his decision to refuse treatment for a type of leukaemia. The information generated by Perplexity was misleading, according to three doctors that Ben consulted. “Do you really think you know more than all of them because of this stupid AI report?” he asked his father. “Yes,” Joe replied. Joe died from cancer late last year. Ben believes psychology played a role in his father’s death but AI wasn’t blameless; it produced misinformation wrapped in scientific packaging.

The New York Times | 18 min read

Mastectomy pain swept under the rug

Sophia Bassan thought she was losing her mind when she experienced debilitating pain for months after undergoing a mastectomy. “One time I was in so much pain that I had to take off my top, and then my cat’s tail brushed against my back. I screamed,” she says. Bassan had to sleep sitting up because lying down was too painful. Post-mastectomy pain syndrome affects an estimated 10-50% of people who undergo the procedure and can last for years. But women are often not warned about this risk by their surgeons and feel dismissed when they have complications. “Doctors act like it is so rare, like they’re so baffled,” says Bassan. “But this is statistically predictable.”

CNN | 10 min read

More than 100

Cancer cells in the blood often form clumps of between two and more than 100 cells to protect themselves against the hostile environment. Animal studies of breast cancer suggest that these groupings, known as circulating tumor cell clusters, could be 50–100 times more likely to produce metastases than are single cells. (Science | 18 min read)

Infographic of the week
Researchers have eliminated a type of deadly brain tumour called glioblastoma in mice using a virus carrying engineered DNA. The DNA encoded a toxin and an immune-stimulating molecule inside cancer cells. But, because the DNA requires cancer-specific proteins called SOX2 and SOX9 in order to be expressed, healthy cells are unaffected by the treatment. (Nature News & Views | 6 min read)
Reference: Nature paper (8 April)

As a kid, I remember spending an entire afternoon watching an ants’ nest with fascination. I always wondered what could be learned through simple observation. It turns out, quite a lot! Entomologist Mark Moffett discovered that small ants called cone ants act like a carwash for the bigger, red harvester ants, just by watching and photographing their interactions. The smaller ants nibble at the larger ants, possibly getting a snack in exchange for their cleaning services.

Send childhood memories, tips and feedback to cancerbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Felicity Nelson, editor, Nature Briefing: Cancer
